failure_modes.text: Teams can optimize for appearing secure instead of being secure when stakeholder incentives are misaligned with the measured outcome.
failure_modes.source_quote: cybersecurity-thinking-models conflicts with incentives (incentives) when stakeholder incentives are not aligned with the security outcome being measured, so defenders optimize for appearing secure rather than being secure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish appearing secure from being secure: audit whether the measured outcomes align with actual threat mitigation, not just compliance optics.

failure_modes.text: Threat modeling can become decorative if it never touches asset specificity, ownership, or operational failure chains.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams fetishize threat models, simulations, or mental frameworks but skip asset specificity, attack surface reality, or operational ownership.
failure_modes.mitigation: Anchor threat modeling to asset specificity, ownership, and operational failure chains; decorative models that never touch real attack surfaces are waste.

failure_modes.text: Security decisions can become static and underfit reality when they are built from compliance diagrams rather than realistic adversarial scenarios.
failure_modes.source_quote: Best used when security decisions must be pressure-tested against realistic scenarios instead of static compliance or architecture diagrams alone.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build security decisions from realistic adversarial scenarios rather than static compliance diagrams; threat landscapes shift faster than checklists.

premortem_questions.text: Which stakeholder gains if this plan underperforms quietly rather than fails visibly?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Every plan has a stakeholder whose incentives are not fully aligned with the outcome you are measuring.

premortem_questions.text: What failure chain crosses technical, human, and governance layers if an attacker chooses the most inconvenient path?
premortem_questions.source_quote: cascading across technical, human, and governance layers at once

premortem_questions.text: Are we modeling realistic attacker incentives or only listing controls we already know how to talk about?
premortem_questions.source_quote: model attacker incentives, failure chains, and cross-domain consequences

heuristics.text: Threat-model the plan against adversarial incentives before treating the architecture as sufficient protection.
Use scenario simulation to test ownership gaps and hidden dependencies, not just control lists.
heuristics.source_quote: test ideas at the speed of compute and anticipate outcomes with unprecedented clarity

heuristics.text: Name the misaligned actor and fallback path before claiming the plan is robust.
heuristics.source_quote: Name that party, describe what they stand to gain, and identify which dependency on them has no fallback.

curation_notes.summary: Focused on adversarial scenario pressure rather than on generic security or compliance vocabulary.
donor_drops: Dropped broader donor-style elaboration in favor of three compact source-grounded intervention chunks per type.
open_questions: Relation richness stays in Wave 3; Wave 2 only preserves the intervention surface needed for preview substrate use.

allies: Wave 3 preserves the first-order ally surface where the raw source gives a clean current-vocabulary seam.
antagonists: Wave 3 preserves the strongest current-vocabulary conflicts and leaves weaker cross-model seams omitted on purpose.
structured_tensions: Wave 4 remains observational only; this batch does not widen higher-order source coverage.
